## _index.md

# Fathom Documentation

> A full developer-documentation refresh (Diátaxis IA, generated SDK references, `llms.txt`, JSON Schemas, Postman collection, VSCode snippets) is planned. Design spec: [`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-15-developer-docs-design.md`](superpowers/specs/2026-04-15-developer-docs-design.md). This index covers the current docs until that work lands.

## Getting Started

- [Getting Started](getting-started.md) — Installation, first template, first rule, first evaluation

## Architecture

- [Core Primitives](core/primitives.md) — Templates, Facts, Rules, Modules, Functions
- [CLIPS Runtime](core/runtime.md) — Embedded CLIPS engine, Engine class API, session lifecycle
- [YAML Compiler](core/yaml-compiler.md) — YAML-to-CLIPS compilation, validation, raw passthrough
- [Fact Asserter](core/fact-asserter.md) — Typed fact insertion, bulk assert, query, retract
- [Forward Chaining](core/forward-chaining.md) — Data-driven inference, Rete algorithm, evaluate cycle
- [Working Memory](core/working-memory.md) — Session state, cumulative reasoning, temporal patterns
- [Audit Log](core/audit-log.md) — Immutable evaluation records, reasoning traces, JSON format
- [Attestation Service](core/attestation.md) — Ed25519 signing, JWT verification

## YAML Authoring Reference

- [YAML Templates](yaml/yaml-templates.md) — Slot definitions, types, validation, defaults, raw CLIPS passthrough
- [YAML Facts](yaml/yaml-facts.md) — Asserting, querying, retracting, and validating facts
- [YAML Rules](yaml/yaml-rule-language.md) — Operators, actions, cross-fact references, rule packs
- [YAML Modules](yaml/yaml-modules.md) — Focus stack, isolation, multi-pack composition
- [YAML Functions](yaml/yaml-functions.md) — Classification operators, temporal operators, raw CLIPS passthrough

## Guides

- [Writing Rules](writing-rules.md) — Practical guide: conditions, salience, modules, raw CLIPS passthrough
- [Integration Overview](integration.md) — Library, sidecar, MCP, and framework adapter usage

## Integrations

- [REST Sidecar](integrations/sidecar.md) — FastAPI server, auth, path-jailing, `/v1/evaluate`, `/v1/compile`
- [gRPC Server](integrations/grpc.md) — gRPC service, bearer-token auth, `protos/fathom.proto`
- [MCP Tool Server](integrations/mcp.md) — `FathomMCPServer`, exposed tools, MCP agent connectivity
- [LangChain Adapter](integrations/langchain.md) — Callback handler for LangChain agent governance
- [CLI](integrations/cli.md) — `fathom validate`, `test`, `bench`, `info`, `repl`
- [Prometheus Metrics](integrations/prometheus.md) — `/metrics` endpoint, counters, histograms
- [Go SDK](integrations/go-sdk.md) — `fathom-go` REST client; gRPC regeneration in progress
- [TypeScript SDK](integrations/typescript-sdk.md) — `@fathom-rules/sdk` v0.1.0

### Planned framework adapters

- [CrewAI Adapter](integrations/crew-ai.md) — Task decorator for CrewAI policy enforcement (planned)
- [OpenAI Agents SDK](integrations/open-ai-agent-sdk.md) — Middleware for OpenAI Agents SDK governance (planned)
- [Google ADK Plugin](integrations/google-adk.md) — Plugin for Google ADK pipeline governance (planned)

## Rule Packs

- [OWASP Agentic](rule-packs/owasp-agentic.md) — OWASP Agentic Top 10 risk mitigations
- [NIST 800-53](rule-packs/nist-ai-rmf.md) — Access control, audit, and information flow controls
- [HIPAA](rule-packs/hipaa.md) — PHI handling, minimum necessary, breach triggers
- [CMMC](rule-packs/cmmc.md) — CMMC Level 2+ controls for agent operations

## Advanced Features

- [Rule Assertions](advanced/rule-assertions.md) — `then.assert` + `bind` + `Engine.register_function`
- [Fleet Reasoning](advanced/fleet.md) — Shared working memory across sessions (Redis, Postgres)
- [Auth & Path Jailing](advanced/auth.md) — Bearer tokens, rule-path allow-lists for REST and gRPC

## CLIPS Features Not in v1

- [Backward Chaining](core/backward-chaining.md) — Goal-driven reasoning (deferred to v2)
- [COOL](core/cool.md) — CLIPS Object-Oriented Language (deferred)
- [Message Handlers](core/message-handlers.md) — Methods on COOL classes (deferred)

## Known limitations

- `fathom-matches` runs user-supplied regexes with no execution budget.
  Rule authors are responsible for vetting pattern complexity. ReDoS-prone
  patterns (e.g. `(a+)+b`) can block the evaluator. Post-auth (C1) this
  is an insider-risk issue, not an attack surface.


## getting-started.md

# Fathom -- Getting Started

Fathom is a deterministic reasoning runtime built on CLIPS via clipspy. Define rules in YAML, evaluate in microseconds, get auditable results with zero hallucinations.

## Installation

Requires Python 3.14 or later.

```bash
uv add fathom-rules
```

The only core dependency is `clipspy`. No other external packages are required.

## Create Your First Template

Templates define the shape of facts. Create a file `templates/agent.yaml`:

```yaml
templates:
  - name: agent
    description: "An AI agent requesting access or action"
    slots:
      - name: id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: clearance
        type: symbol
        required: true
        allowed_values: [unclassified, cui, confidential, secret, top-secret]
      - name: purpose
        type: symbol
        required: true
      - name: session_id
        type: string
        required: true

  - name: data_request
    description: "A request to access a data source"
    slots:
      - name: agent_id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: target
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: classification
        type: symbol
        required: true
      - name: action
        type: symbol
        allowed_values: [read, write, delete]
        default: read
```

Each slot has a `type` (`string`, `symbol`, `float`) and optional constraints (`required`, `allowed_values`, `default`). Fathom validates facts against these schemas at assertion time.

## Write Your First Rule

Rules define pattern-matching logic that fires when facts match conditions. Create `rules/access-control.yaml`:

```yaml
ruleset: access-control
version: 1.0
module: governance

rules:
  - name: deny-insufficient-clearance
    description: "Deny access when agent clearance is below data classification"
    salience: 10
    when:
      agent:
        clearance: below($data.classification)
      data_request:
        as: $data
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Agent clearance '{agent.clearance}' insufficient for '{$data.classification}' data"
      log: full
```

`salience` controls firing priority -- higher values fire first. The evaluator uses last-write-wins on `__fathom_decision` facts, so the rule firing LAST wins. Deny rules should have LOWER salience than allow rules so they fire last and override any prior allow, enforcing fail-closed behavior.

## Set Up Modules

Modules namespace rules into isolated groups with controlled execution order. Create `modules.yaml`:

```yaml
modules:
  - name: classification
    description: "Classification and clearance evaluation"
    priority: 1

  - name: governance
    description: "Action-level governance rules"
    priority: 2

focus_order: [classification, governance]
```

The `focus_order` list determines evaluation sequence. Rules in `classification` fire before rules in `governance`. Each rule file declares which module it belongs to via the `module` field.

## Run Your First Evaluation

```python
from fathom import Engine

engine = Engine()
engine.load_templates("templates/")
engine.load_modules("modules.yaml")
engine.load_rules("rules/access-control.yaml")

engine.assert_fact("agent", {
    "id": "agent-alpha",
    "clearance": "secret",
    "purpose": "threat-analysis",
    "session_id": "sess-001"
})

engine.assert_fact("data_request", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha",
    "target": "hr_records",
    "classification": "top-secret",
    "action": "read"
})

result = engine.evaluate()

print(result.decision)     # "deny"
print(result.reason)       # "Agent clearance 'secret' insufficient for 'top-secret' data"
print(result.rule_trace)   # ["classification::resolve-levels", "governance::deny-insufficient-clearance"]
print(result.module_trace) # ["classification", "governance"]
print(result.duration_us)  # 47
```

The result object contains five fields:

- `decision` -- the action taken (`allow`, `deny`, `escalate`, etc.)
- `reason` -- human-readable explanation with interpolated variables
- `rule_trace` -- ordered list of rules that fired, prefixed by module name
- `module_trace` -- ordered list of modules that were evaluated
- `duration_us` -- evaluation time in microseconds

Working memory persists across evaluations within a session. This enables cumulative reasoning ("agent accessed PII from 3 sources -- deny the 4th") and temporal patterns that stateless engines cannot express.

## Next Steps

- [Core Primitives](core/primitives.md) -- templates, facts, rules, modules, and functions in depth
- [YAML Rule Language](yaml/yaml-rule-language.md) -- operators, conditions, actions, and rule packs
- [Working Memory](core/working-memory.md) -- session state, queries, cumulative reasoning
- [Integration](integration.md) -- library, sidecar, MCP tool, and framework adapters

## Do Not

- Do not skip template definitions and assert untyped facts -- Fathom validates at assertion time.
- Do not set deny rules to higher salience than allow rules -- deny must have lower salience so it fires last and wins under last-write-wins (fail-closed).
- Do not assume working memory resets between `evaluate()` calls within the same session -- it persists.
- Do not use raw CLIPS constructs when YAML equivalents exist -- YAML is the primary authoring interface.


## tutorials/index.md

# Tutorials

1. [Hello-world policy](hello-world.md) — install Fathom, write your first rule, evaluate a fact.
2. [Modules & salience](modules-and-salience.md) — split rules across modules, control execution order.
3. [Working memory across evaluations](working-memory.md) — persist facts between `evaluate()` calls.

Each tutorial takes ~20 minutes and assumes no prior CLIPS or Fathom knowledge.


## tutorials/hello-world.md

# Hello-world policy

In this tutorial you'll install Fathom, define one template and one rule in YAML, load them into an engine, assert a fact, and read the evaluation result that comes back.

## 1. Install

```bash no-verify
pip install fathom-rules
```

## 2. Define a template

A template is the schema for a fact. Save this as `agent.yaml`:

```yaml
templates:
  - name: agent
    slots:
      - name: id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: clearance
        type: symbol
        required: true
        allowed_values: [public, confidential, secret]
```

## 3. Define a rule

Save this as `rules.yaml`:

```yaml
ruleset: demo
version: "1.0"
module: MAIN
rules:
  - name: allow-public
    when:
      - template: agent
        conditions:
          - slot: clearance
            expression: "== public"
    then:
      action: allow
```

## 4. Load and evaluate

```python no-verify
from fathom.engine import Engine

engine = Engine()
engine.load_templates("agent.yaml")
engine.load_rules("rules.yaml")

engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "a-1", "clearance": "public"})
result = engine.evaluate()

print(result.decision)      # -> "allow"
print(result.rule_trace)    # -> ["allow-public"]
```

The `no-verify` tag skips snippet execution because the install step and file paths aren't part of the test harness. The engine calls themselves are verified in [working memory](working-memory.md), which builds on this example with a real in-memory path.

## What just happened?

- Fathom compiled your YAML to CLIPS constructs via `fathom.compiler.Compiler` and loaded them into an embedded CLIPS environment.
- Your fact matched the condition `clearance == public`, the rule fired, and the rule's `then.action: allow` became the decision on the evaluation result.
- The `EvaluationResult` captures which rules fired (`rule_trace`), the final `decision`, and the evaluation duration — see [Audit & Attestation](../concepts/audit-attestation.md).

## Next

- [Modules & salience](modules-and-salience.md) — add a deny rule with lower salience so it fires last and wins.


## tutorials/modules-and-salience.md

# Modules & salience

This tutorial builds on [Hello-world policy](hello-world.md). You will add a
deny rule alongside an allow rule, tune their salience so the deny always wins,
and confirm the fail-closed outcome.

## How salience and last-write-wins interact

Fathom compiles your YAML to CLIPS via `fathom.compiler.Compiler`. CLIPS fires
eligible rules in salience order — the **highest** salience rule fires **first**.
The `Evaluator` uses a **last-write-wins** strategy: the final
`__fathom_decision` fact asserted into working memory becomes the result that
`Engine.evaluate` returns as `EvaluationResult.decision`.

Put those two facts together and the fail-closed design becomes clear:

- Give the **allow** rule a **high** salience (e.g. 100) so it fires first.
- Give the **deny** rule a **low** salience (e.g. 10) so it fires after.
- Because the deny fact is written last, it overwrites the allow fact and wins.

`RuleDefinition.salience` (in `src/fathom/models.py`) is an `int` that defaults
to `0`. Any positive integer is valid.

## 1. Install

```bash no-verify
pip install fathom-rules
```

## 2. Define a template

Save as `agent.yaml`:

```yaml no-verify
templates:
  - name: agent
    slots:
      - name: id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: clearance
        type: symbol
        required: true
        allowed_values: [public, confidential, secret]
```

## 3. Define a module

Rules in Fathom must belong to a named module. `Compiler.compile_module`
(in `src/fathom/compiler.py`) emits the CLIPS `defmodule` construct; modules are
loaded into the engine with `Engine.load_modules`.

Save as `modules.yaml`:

```yaml no-verify
modules:
  - name: governance
    description: Access-control governance layer
focus_order:
  - governance
```

The optional `focus_order` list tells the engine which modules to activate and
in what order. Internally, `Compiler.compile_focus_stack` reverses this list
before building the CLIPS `(focus ...)` command because CLIPS uses push
semantics — the last module pushed ends up on top of the execution stack and
therefore runs first.

## 4. Define two rules with different salience

Both rules match an agent whose `clearance` slot equals `public`. The allow
rule fires first (`salience: 100`); the deny rule fires second (`salience: 10`)
and overwrites the allow decision via last-write-wins.

Save as `rules.yaml`:

```yaml no-verify
module: governance
rules:
  - name: allow-public
    salience: 100
    when:
      - template: agent
        conditions:
          - slot: clearance
            expression: "equals(public)"
    then:
      action: allow

  - name: deny-public
    salience: 10
    when:
      - template: agent
        conditions:
          - slot: clearance
            expression: "equals(public)"
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Public clearance is not sufficient"
```

## 5. Load, assert, and evaluate

The Python block below writes the three YAML definitions to a temporary
directory, loads them in the required order (templates → modules → rules),
asserts a fact, and verifies the deny outcome.

```python
import pathlib, tempfile
from fathom.engine import Engine

TEMPLATES_YAML = """
templates:
  - name: agent
    slots:
      - name: id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: clearance
        type: symbol
        required: true
        allowed_values: [public, confidential, secret]
"""

MODULES_YAML = """
modules:
  - name: governance
    description: Access-control governance layer
focus_order:
  - governance
"""

RULES_YAML = """
module: governance
rules:
  - name: allow-public
    salience: 100
    when:
      - template: agent
        conditions:
          - slot: clearance
            expression: "equals(public)"
    then:
      action: allow

  - name: deny-public
    salience: 10
    when:
      - template: agent
        conditions:
          - slot: clearance
            expression: "equals(public)"
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Public clearance is not sufficient"
"""

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp:
    d = pathlib.Path(tmp)
    (d / "agent.yaml").write_text(TEMPLATES_YAML)
    (d / "modules.yaml").write_text(MODULES_YAML)
    (d / "rules.yaml").write_text(RULES_YAML)

    engine = Engine()
    engine.load_templates(str(d / "agent.yaml"))
    engine.load_modules(str(d / "modules.yaml"))
    engine.load_rules(str(d / "rules.yaml"))

    engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "a-1", "clearance": "public"})
    result = engine.evaluate()

    assert result.decision == "deny", f"expected deny, got {result.decision!r}"
    assert "governance::deny-public" in result.rule_trace, (
        f"rule_trace was {result.rule_trace}"
    )
```

Both rules match. `allow-public` fires first (salience 100) and writes an allow
fact. `deny-public` fires second (salience 10) and writes a deny fact. Because
the evaluator uses last-write-wins, the deny fact is the winner.

`result.decision` is `"deny"` and `result.rule_trace` includes
`"governance::deny-public"` (rules are recorded as `module::rule_name`).

## What just happened?

- **Modules** give rules a namespace. `Compiler.compile_module` emits
  `(defmodule governance (import MAIN ?ALL))` so governance rules can reference
  the internal decision template defined in `MAIN`.
- **Focus stack** — `Compiler.compile_focus_stack(["governance"])` produces
  `(focus governance)`. The evaluator pushes that onto the CLIPS agenda before
  running the forward-chain cycle.
- **Salience** controls firing order. Lower-salience rules fire later. The last
  decision fact written wins, so the deny rule at salience 10 overrides the
  allow rule at salience 100. This is the fail-closed design: allow rules can
  only succeed when no deny rule fires after them.
- `EvaluationResult.decision` and `EvaluationResult.rule_trace` (defined in
  `src/fathom/models.py`) capture the outcome.

## Next

- Explore [Working memory](working-memory.md) to see how facts persist across
  multiple evaluations within a session.


## core/primitives.md

# Runtime — Core Primitives

Fathom exposes five primitives from CLIPS. Everything else (rule packs, governance, routing) composes from these: Templates, Facts, Rules, Modules, and Functions.

## What It Is

The core primitives are the building blocks of Fathom's deterministic reasoning engine. Each maps to a CLIPS construct, authored in YAML (compiled to CLIPS) or via raw CLIPS passthrough. The engine is embedded via `clipspy`.

## Templates

Templates define fact schemas (`deftemplate`). Each template has named slots with types, constraints, and defaults. The YAML compiler validates facts against templates at assertion time.

**Slot properties:** `name`, `type` (string, symbol, float), `required` (boolean), `allowed_values` (list), `default` (value).

```yaml
# templates/agent.yaml
templates:
  - name: agent
    slots:
      - name: id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: clearance
        type: symbol
        required: true
        allowed_values: [unclassified, cui, confidential, secret, top-secret]
      - name: purpose
        type: symbol
        required: true
      - name: session_id
        type: string
        required: true
```

```python
engine = Engine()
engine.load_templates("templates/")

engine.assert_fact("agent", {
    "id": "agent-alpha",
    "clearance": "secret",
    "purpose": "threat-analysis",
    "session_id": "sess-001"
})
# Raises ValidationError for unknown slots or disallowed values
```

## Facts

Facts are typed instances of templates asserted into working memory. Working memory persists across evaluations within a session, enabling cumulative reasoning and temporal patterns.

**Operations:** `assert_fact`, `assert_facts` (bulk), `query`, `count`, `retract`.

```python
engine.assert_facts([
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "a1", "source": "hr_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020400.0}),
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "a1", "source": "finance_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020460.0}),
])

agents = engine.query("agent", {"clearance": "secret"})
pii_count = engine.count("access_log", {"agent_id": "a1", "data_type": "PII"})
engine.retract("data_request", {"agent_id": "a1", "target": "hr_records"})
```

## Rules

Rules define pattern-matching logic (`defrule`). Authored in YAML with `when`/`then` blocks, compiled to CLIPS. Each rule belongs to a module and has a salience (priority).

**Salience:** Higher fires first. The evaluator uses last-write-wins, so the rule firing LAST determines the outcome. Deny rules get LOWER salience than allow rules so they fire last and win (fail-closed default).

**Supported condition operators:** `equals`, `not_equals`, `greater_than`, `less_than`, `in`, `not_in`, `contains`, `matches`, `below`, `meets_or_exceeds`, `within_scope`, `changed_within`, `count_exceeds`, `rate_exceeds`, and cross-fact references via `$other_fact.field`.

**Supported actions:** `allow`, `deny`, `escalate`, `scope`, `route`. Each action supports `reason` (with `{variable}` interpolation), `log` (none/summary/full), `notify`, `attestation`, and `metadata`.

```yaml
# rules/access-control.yaml
ruleset: access-control
module: governance

rules:
  - name: deny-insufficient-clearance
    salience: 10
    when:
      agent:
        clearance: below($data.classification)
      data_request:
        as: $data
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Agent clearance '{agent.clearance}' insufficient for '{$data.classification}' data"
      log: full

  - name: allow-matching-clearance
    salience: 100
    when:
      agent:
        clearance: meets_or_exceeds($data.classification)
        purpose: in($data.allowed_purposes)
      data_request:
        as: $data
    then:
      action: allow
      scope: $data.authorized_collections
      log: full
```

## Modules

Modules namespace rules into isolated groups (`defmodule`). The focus stack controls execution order. Each module has a priority; `focus_order` determines which fires first.

```yaml
# modules.yaml
modules:
  - name: classification
    priority: 1
  - name: governance
    priority: 2
  - name: routing
    priority: 3
  - name: cumulative
    priority: 4

focus_order: [classification, governance, routing, cumulative]
```

```python
engine.load_modules("modules.yaml")
engine.load_rules("rules/classification.yaml")  # module: classification
engine.load_rules("rules/access-control.yaml")  # module: governance

result = engine.evaluate()
print(result.module_trace)  # ["classification", "governance"]
```

Multiple rule packs compose safely because each declares its module. NIST rules in `nist`, HIPAA in `hipaa`. No name collisions.

## Functions

Functions provide reusable logic (`deffunction`). Two categories: classification-aware operators (`below`, `meets_or_exceeds`) and temporal operators (`count_exceeds`, `rate_exceeds`).

```yaml
# functions/classification.yaml
functions:
  - name: below
    params: [level_a, level_b]
    hierarchy_ref: classification.yaml

  - name: meets_or_exceeds
    params: [level_a, level_b]
    hierarchy_ref: classification.yaml

  - name: count_exceeds
    params: [template, filter, threshold]

  - name: rate_exceeds
    params: [template, filter, threshold, window_seconds]
```

Raw CLIPS passthrough for advanced cases:

```python
engine.load_clips_function("""
(deffunction combined-risk (?clearance ?data-class ?access-count)
    (bind ?base-risk (- (classification-rank ?data-class) (classification-rank ?clearance)))
    (bind ?volume-risk (/ ?access-count 10))
    (+ ?base-risk ?volume-risk))
""")
```

## Evaluation Result

`engine.evaluate()` returns a result with: `decision`, `reason`, `rule_trace` (list of `module::rule` strings), `module_trace` (modules traversed), and `duration_us` (microseconds).

```python
result = engine.evaluate()
print(result.decision)     # "deny"
print(result.rule_trace)   # ["classification::resolve-levels", "governance::deny-insufficient-clearance"]
print(result.duration_us)  # 47
```

## Conventions

- YAML is the primary authoring format; raw CLIPS passthrough is available for all construct types.
- Templates live in `templates/` directory, rules in `rules/`, functions in `functions/`, modules in `modules.yaml`.
- Every rule declares its `module` in the YAML file header.
- Salience values: allow rules use higher salience (e.g., 100) than deny rules (e.g., 10) — deny fires last and wins under last-write-wins.
- Working memory is stateful within a session, stateless across sessions.
- The YAML compiler handles type checking, variable binding, slot validation, and module assignment at compile time.

## Do Not

- Do not use COOL (CLIPS Object System), backward chaining, generic functions, or message handlers. These are explicitly excluded from v1.
- Do not rely on rule firing order without explicit salience values. Always set salience on rules where order matters.
- Do not assert facts without loading the corresponding template first. The engine validates against loaded templates.
- Do not assume working memory resets between evaluations within the same session. It persists.
- Do not skip module assignment in rule YAML files. Every rule must belong to a module for safe composition.


## core/runtime.md

# Runtime -- CLIPS Runtime

## What It Is

The CLIPS Runtime is the embedded CLIPS engine at the heart of Fathom. It is loaded via `clipspy` (the Python binding to the CLIPS C library) and manages working memory, the template registry, the module focus stack, rule activation, and forward-chaining inference. The runtime is stateful within a session and stateless across sessions.

The runtime is wrapped by the `Engine` class, which provides the public Python API for loading constructs, asserting facts, and triggering evaluation.

## Engine Class API

The `Engine` class is the primary interface to the CLIPS Runtime.

### Construction

```python
from fathom import Engine

# Default construction -- empty engine, no templates or rules loaded
engine = Engine()

# Convenience constructor -- loads all rules from a directory
engine = Engine.from_rules("rules/")
```

### Loading Constructs

All loading methods accept file paths or directory paths. Directories are scanned for `.yaml` files.

```python
engine.load_templates("templates/")       # YAML -> deftemplate
engine.load_modules("modules.yaml")       # YAML -> defmodule + focus stack
engine.load_functions("functions/")       # YAML -> deffunction
engine.load_rules("rules/")              # YAML -> defrule
```

Raw CLIPS passthrough is available for all construct types:

```python
engine.load_clips_function("""
(deffunction combined-risk (?clearance ?data-class ?access-count)
    (bind ?base-risk (- (classification-rank ?data-class) (classification-rank ?clearance)))
    (bind ?volume-risk (/ ?access-count 10))
    (+ ?base-risk ?volume-risk))
""")
```

### Fact Operations

```python
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "a1", "clearance": "secret", ...})
engine.assert_facts([("access_log", {...}), ("access_log", {...})])
engine.retract("data_request", {"agent_id": "a1", "target": "hr_records"})

agents = engine.query("agent", {"clearance": "secret"})
count = engine.count("access_log", {"agent_id": "a1", "data_type": "PII"})
```

### Evaluation

```python
result = engine.evaluate()

result.decision      # "deny" | "allow" | "escalate" | "scope" | "route"
result.reason        # Human-readable explanation
result.rule_trace    # ["classification::resolve-levels", "governance::deny-insufficient-clearance"]
result.module_trace  # ["classification", "governance"]
result.duration_us   # Microseconds elapsed
```

## Internal Components

The runtime manages five subsystems internally:

**Template Registry** -- stores all loaded `deftemplate` definitions. Every `assert_fact` call validates against this registry. Templates are immutable once loaded.

**Working Memory** -- the fact store. Facts persist across `evaluate()` calls within a session. Bounded to less than 5MB for 1000 facts. Cleared when the session ends.

**Module Focus Stack** -- an ordered stack of `defmodule` entries. When `evaluate()` runs, CLIPS processes modules in focus-stack order. Each module's rules fire to completion before the next module begins. The `focus_order` from `modules.yaml` determines the initial stack.

**Rule Activation** -- the Rete network. When facts enter working memory, the Rete algorithm incrementally updates partial matches. When `evaluate()` is called, activated rules fire in salience order within each module.

**Forward-Chaining Inference** -- the execution model. Facts drive rule activation (data-driven). Rules that fire may assert new facts, which trigger further rules. This continues until no more rules can fire (quiescence). See [Forward Chaining](forward-chaining.md).

## Session Lifecycle

1. Create an `Engine` instance (empty working memory, no constructs).
2. Load templates, modules, functions, and rules (compiled once via the YAML compiler).
3. Assert facts into working memory.
4. Call `engine.evaluate()` -- rules fire, result returned.
5. Optionally assert more facts and evaluate again (working memory accumulates).
6. Session ends when the `Engine` instance is discarded. Working memory is lost.

## Performance

| Operation | Target |
|---|---|
| Single rule evaluation | < 100 microseconds |
| 100-rule evaluation | < 500 microseconds |
| Fact assertion | < 10 microseconds |
| YAML compilation | < 50ms (one-time) |
| Working memory (1000 facts) | < 5MB |

## Conventions

- One `Engine` instance per session. Do not share instances across threads without synchronization.
- Load all constructs before asserting facts. Template validation depends on the registry being populated.
- Use `Engine.from_rules()` for simple cases. Use explicit `load_*` methods when you need control over load order.
- The CLIPS runtime is embedded, not a separate process. No IPC overhead.
- `clipspy` is the only external dependency beyond the Python standard library.

## Do Not

- Do not share an `Engine` instance across threads. CLIPS is not thread-safe.
- Do not load constructs after evaluation has started. Load all templates, modules, functions, and rules before the first `assert_fact`.
- Do not assume working memory resets between `evaluate()` calls. It persists within the session.
- Do not call `evaluate()` to inspect state. Use `query()` or `count()` instead.
- Do not use COOL, backward chaining, generic functions, or message handlers. These are excluded from v1.


## core/yaml-compiler.md

# Runtime -- YAML Compiler

## What It Is

The YAML Compiler translates YAML definitions of templates, rules, modules, and functions into CLIPS constructs (`deftemplate`, `defrule`, `defmodule`, `deffunction`). It performs type checking, variable binding resolution, slot validation, and module assignment at compile time. Compilation is a one-time cost (target: less than 50ms) that runs when constructs are loaded into the engine.

Raw CLIPS passthrough is supported for all construct types, allowing advanced users to write CLIPS directly when the YAML abstraction cannot express the needed logic.

## Compilation Targets

| YAML Source | CLIPS Construct | Engine Method |
|---|---|---|
| Template definitions | `deftemplate` | `engine.load_templates()` |
| Rule definitions | `defrule` | `engine.load_rules()` |
| Module definitions | `defmodule` | `engine.load_modules()` |
| Function definitions | `deffunction` | `engine.load_functions()` |

## Compile-Time Validation

The compiler catches errors before any facts enter working memory.

**Type checking** -- slot types declared in templates (`string`, `symbol`, `float`) are enforced. A rule referencing a slot with an incompatible operator produces a compile error.

**Slot validation** -- rules that reference slots not present in a template are rejected. Typos like `clearence` instead of `clearance` fail at compile time, not at runtime.

**Variable binding** -- cross-fact references (`$data.classification`) are resolved and validated. The compiler verifies that the aliased fact template (`as: $data`) exists and the referenced slot is defined.

**Module assignment** -- every rule YAML file declares a `module` in its header. The compiler verifies the module has been defined and assigns the rule to it. Rules without a module declaration are rejected.

**Allowed values** -- if a template slot has `allowed_values`, conditions referencing literal values against that slot are checked at compile time.

## Template Compilation

```yaml
templates:
  - name: agent
    slots:
      - name: id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: clearance
        type: symbol
        required: true
        allowed_values: [unclassified, cui, confidential, secret, top-secret]
```

Compiles to a CLIPS `deftemplate` with typed slots and constraints. The `required` flag ensures facts missing that slot are rejected at assertion time.

## Rule Compilation

```yaml
ruleset: access-control
module: governance

rules:
  - name: deny-insufficient-clearance
    salience: 100
    when:
      agent:
        clearance: below($data.classification)
      data_request:
        as: $data
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Agent clearance insufficient for data classification"
      log: full
```

The compiler translates `when` blocks into CLIPS pattern-matching expressions and `then` blocks into rule actions. The `salience` value becomes the CLIPS rule salience. The `module` header assigns the generated `defrule` to the correct `defmodule`.

**Supported condition operators:** `equals`, `not_equals`, `greater_than`, `less_than`, `in`, `not_in`, `contains`, `matches`, `below`, `meets_or_exceeds`, `within_scope`, `changed_within`, `count_exceeds`, `rate_exceeds`, and cross-fact references via `$alias.field`.

**Supported actions:** `allow`, `deny`, `escalate`, `scope`, `route`. Each supports `reason` (with `{variable}` interpolation), `log` (none/summary/full), `notify`, `attestation`, and `metadata`.

## Module Compilation

```yaml
modules:
  - name: classification
    priority: 1
  - name: governance
    priority: 2

focus_order: [classification, governance]
```

Each module compiles to a `defmodule`. The `focus_order` array sets the initial module focus stack in the CLIPS runtime.

## Function Compilation

```yaml
functions:
  - name: below
    params: [level_a, level_b]
    hierarchy_ref: classification.yaml
```

Compiles to a CLIPS `deffunction`. The `hierarchy_ref` tells the compiler where to find the classification hierarchy used by the function's logic.

## Raw CLIPS Passthrough

For any construct type, raw CLIPS can be loaded directly. This bypasses the YAML compiler entirely.

```python
engine.load_clips_function("""
(deffunction combined-risk (?clearance ?data-class ?access-count)
    (bind ?base-risk (- (classification-rank ?data-class) (classification-rank ?clearance)))
    (bind ?volume-risk (/ ?access-count 10))
    (+ ?base-risk ?volume-risk))
""")
```

Use raw CLIPS for complex math, string manipulation, custom scoring, or any logic the YAML abstraction cannot express.

## Conventions

- YAML is the primary authoring format. Use raw CLIPS only when YAML cannot express the logic.
- Templates in `templates/`, rules in `rules/`, functions in `functions/`, modules in `modules.yaml`.
- Every rule YAML file must include a `module` header.
- Compilation runs once at load time. There is no re-compilation during evaluation.
- The compiler targets less than 50ms for a full rule pack compilation.

## Do Not

- Do not mix YAML and raw CLIPS for the same construct. A template is either YAML-authored or raw CLIPS, not both.
- Do not skip module declarations in rule files. The compiler rejects rules without a module.
- Do not reference slots or templates that have not been loaded. The compiler validates references at compile time.
- Do not assume compilation order matters within a single `load_*` call. All constructs in a directory are compiled as a batch.
- Do not hand-edit generated CLIPS output. The compiler is the source of truth for YAML-authored constructs.


## core/fact-asserter.md

# Runtime -- Fact Asserter

## What It Is

The Fact Asserter handles typed fact insertion into working memory, validated against loaded templates. It supports single and bulk assertion, working memory queries (`engine.query`, `engine.count`), and fact retraction (`engine.retract`). Assertion targets less than 10 microseconds per fact.

Facts are typed instances of templates. The asserter validates every fact against the template registry before inserting it into working memory. Invalid facts (unknown slots, disallowed values, missing required fields) are rejected with a `ValidationError`.

## API

### Single Assertion

```python
engine.assert_fact("agent", {
    "id": "agent-alpha",
    "clearance": "secret",
    "purpose": "threat-analysis",
    "session_id": "sess-001"
})
```

The first argument is the template name. The second is a dictionary of slot-value pairs. The asserter validates:

- The template exists in the registry.
- All keys are valid slot names for that template.
- All `required` slots are present.
- Values match the slot `type` (string, symbol, float).
- Values are within `allowed_values` if the slot defines them.
- Default values are applied for missing optional slots that have defaults.

### Bulk Assertion

```python
engine.assert_facts([
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "a1", "source": "hr_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020400.0}),
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "a1", "source": "finance_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020460.0}),
])
```

Each tuple is `(template_name, slot_dict)`. All facts are validated individually. If any fact fails validation, the entire batch is rejected -- no partial insertion.

### Querying Working Memory

Inspect working memory state without triggering rule evaluation.

```python
# Find all facts matching a template and filter
agents = engine.query("agent", {"clearance": "secret"})

# Count matching facts
pii_count = engine.count("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "data_type": "PII"})
```

`query()` returns a list of matching fact dictionaries. `count()` returns an integer. Neither method fires rules or modifies working memory.

### Fact Retraction

Remove a fact from working memory.

```python
engine.retract("data_request", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "target": "hr_records"})
```

The fact matching the template name and slot values is removed. If no matching fact exists, the call is a no-op.

## Validation Errors

```python
# Unknown slot name
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "a1", "clearence": "secret"})
# -> ValidationError: 'clearence' is not a valid slot for template 'agent'

# Disallowed value
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "a1", "clearance": "cosmic"})
# -> ValidationError: 'cosmic' not in allowed_values for slot 'clearance'

# Missing required slot
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"purpose": "analysis"})
# -> ValidationError: required slot 'id' missing for template 'agent'

# Unknown template
engine.assert_fact("nonexistent", {"key": "value"})
# -> ValidationError: template 'nonexistent' not found in registry
```

## Performance

| Operation | Target |
|---|---|
| Single fact assertion | < 10 microseconds |
| Bulk assertion (per fact) | < 10 microseconds |
| Query | Proportional to working memory size |
| Count | Proportional to working memory size |

## Conventions

- Always load templates before asserting facts. The asserter validates against the template registry.
- Use `assert_facts` for inserting multiple related facts. It is atomic -- all succeed or all fail.
- Use `query()` and `count()` to inspect state. Do not call `evaluate()` just to read working memory.
- Retraction matches by template name and slot values. Be specific enough to match exactly one fact.
- Default values on optional slots are applied automatically when the slot is omitted.

## Do Not

- Do not assert facts before loading templates. The asserter has no schema to validate against.
- Do not catch and ignore `ValidationError`. It indicates a programming error (wrong slot name, bad value).
- Do not assume bulk assertion is partially applied on failure. It is all-or-nothing.
- Do not use `query()` with an empty filter to dump all facts of a type in production. It returns every matching fact in memory.
- Do not rely on assertion order to control rule firing. Rules fire based on salience and module focus order.


## core/forward-chaining.md

# Runtime -- Forward Chaining

## What It Is

Forward chaining is Fathom's primary inference method. It is data-driven: facts asserted into working memory trigger rules whose conditions match those facts. When a rule fires, it may assert new facts, which in turn trigger additional rules. This continues until no more rules can fire (quiescence). Forward chaining is how CLIPS and Fathom evaluate all rules in v1.

## How It Works

The forward-chaining cycle follows this sequence:

1. **Assert facts** -- facts enter working memory via `engine.assert_fact()` or `engine.assert_facts()`.
2. **Pattern matching** -- the Rete algorithm incrementally matches facts against rule conditions. Rules whose conditions are fully satisfied become "activated" and enter the agenda.
3. **Conflict resolution** -- when multiple rules are activated, the engine selects one to fire based on salience (higher fires first) and module focus stack order.
4. **Rule firing** -- the selected rule executes its `then` block (assert new facts, set a decision, log).
5. **Repeat** -- new facts from step 4 re-enter step 2. The cycle continues until the agenda is empty.
6. **Quiescence** -- no more rules can fire. The evaluation result is returned.

```
Assert Facts -> Rete Match -> Agenda -> Fire Rule -> (new facts?) -> Repeat
                                                         |
                                                    No new activations
                                                         |
                                                    Quiescence -> Return Result
```

## The Rete Algorithm

CLIPS uses the Rete algorithm for pattern matching. Rete builds a network of condition nodes that facts flow through. Key properties:

- **Incremental** -- when a new fact is asserted, only the relevant parts of the network are updated. The engine does not re-evaluate all rules from scratch.
- **Shared nodes** -- conditions common to multiple rules share network nodes. If five rules all check `agent.clearance`, that check exists once in the network.
- **Scales well** -- 100-rule evaluation targets less than 500 microseconds because Rete avoids redundant matching.

This is why Fathom can evaluate complex rule sets in microseconds rather than milliseconds.

## The Evaluate Cycle

```python
engine.load_templates("templates/")
engine.load_modules("modules.yaml")
engine.load_rules("rules/")

engine.assert_fact("agent", {
    "id": "agent-alpha",
    "clearance": "secret",
    "purpose": "threat-analysis",
    "session_id": "sess-001"
})
engine.assert_fact("data_request", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha",
    "target": "hr_records",
    "classification": "top-secret",
    "action": "read"
})

result = engine.evaluate()
```

What happens inside `evaluate()`:

1. The engine pushes modules onto the focus stack per `focus_order`: `[classification, governance, routing, cumulative]`.
2. The `classification` module gets focus. Its rules are checked against working memory. `resolve-levels` fires.
3. The `governance` module gets focus. `deny-insufficient-clearance` matches (agent clearance `secret` is below data classification `top-secret`). It fires, setting `decision = deny`.
4. Remaining modules (`routing`, `cumulative`) may or may not get focus depending on whether early termination is configured.
5. The result is returned: `decision="deny"`, `rule_trace=["classification::resolve-levels", "governance::deny-insufficient-clearance"]`.

## Module Focus Stack

Modules control the order in which groups of rules are considered. The focus stack is processed top-to-bottom. Within each module, rules fire in salience order. When a module's agenda is exhausted, the next module on the stack gets focus.

```yaml
focus_order: [classification, governance, routing, cumulative]
```

This guarantees that classification rules fire before governance rules, which fire before routing rules. Each module's output facts are available to subsequent modules.

## Salience

Within a module, salience determines firing order when multiple rules are activated simultaneously.

```yaml
rules:
  - name: allow-matching-clearance
    salience: 100   # fires first
    ...
  - name: deny-insufficient-clearance
    salience: 10    # fires second — last-write-wins means deny wins
    ...
```

Higher salience fires first. The evaluator uses last-write-wins on `__fathom_decision` facts, so the rule firing LAST determines the outcome. Deny rules must have LOWER salience than allow rules so they fire last and enforce a fail-closed default.

## Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Data-driven (facts trigger rules) | Goal-driven (prove a hypothesis) |
| Mechanism | Assert facts, rules fire automatically | Set a goal, engine searches for supporting facts |
| Fathom v1 | Primary inference method | Explicitly excluded |
| Use cases | Governance, routing, classification | Diagnostics, hypothesis testing |

Forward chaining covers all v1 use cases: policy enforcement, data routing, compliance checks, and classification. Backward chaining is a v2 consideration. See [Backward Chaining](backward-chaining.md).

## Conventions

- Forward chaining is the only inference method in v1. All rules are forward-chaining rules.
- Use the module focus stack to control evaluation order across rule groups.
- Use salience to control firing order within a module.
- Rules that assert new facts create inference chains. Keep chains short and predictable.
- The Rete algorithm makes pattern matching efficient. Do not try to optimize by reducing rule count at the expense of clarity.

## Do Not

- Do not create circular inference chains (Rule A asserts a fact that triggers Rule B, which asserts a fact that triggers Rule A). This causes infinite loops.
- Do not rely on implicit rule ordering. Always use salience and modules for explicit control.
- Do not assume all modules are traversed. Evaluation may stop early if a decision is reached.
- Do not use backward chaining in v1. It is not supported.
- Do not assume facts asserted by rules during evaluation are visible to `query()` before `evaluate()` returns.


## core/working-memory.md

# Runtime — Working Memory

## What It Is

Working memory is Fathom's in-session fact store. Facts asserted into working memory persist across multiple `engine.evaluate()` calls within the same session, then disappear when the session ends. This makes Fathom stateful within a session and stateless across sessions.

This is the core differentiator over stateless policy engines like OPA and Cedar, which evaluate a single request against policy and forget everything between calls.

## How It Works

Every call to `engine.assert_fact()` inserts a typed fact into working memory. That fact remains available to all subsequent evaluations until explicitly retracted or the session ends. Rules pattern-match against the full contents of working memory on each evaluation.

```python
from fathom import Engine

engine = Engine()
engine.load_templates("templates/")
engine.load_rules("rules/")

# Fact persists across evaluations
engine.assert_fact("agent", {
    "id": "agent-alpha",
    "clearance": "secret",
    "purpose": "threat-analysis",
    "session_id": "sess-001"
})

result1 = engine.evaluate()  # sees the agent fact

engine.assert_fact("data_request", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha",
    "target": "hr_records",
    "classification": "top-secret",
    "action": "read"
})

result2 = engine.evaluate()  # sees both facts
```

## Cumulative Reasoning

Rules can count facts that have accumulated over a session and act on thresholds. The `count_exceeds` function counts facts matching a template and filter, returning true when the count passes a threshold.

Example: "This agent accessed PII from 3 sources this session -- deny the 4th."

```python
engine.assert_fact("access_log", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "hr_db",
    "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020400.0
})
engine.assert_fact("access_log", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "finance_db",
    "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020460.0
})
engine.assert_fact("access_log", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "medical_db",
    "data_type": "PHI", "timestamp": 1713020520.0
})

# Rule using count_exceeds fires -- 3 PII accesses exceed threshold
result = engine.evaluate()  # -> escalate
```

A YAML rule implementing this pattern:

```yaml
rules:
  - name: excessive-pii-access
    salience: 100
    when:
      access_log:
        agent_id: $agent.id
        data_type: in([PII, PHI])
        data_type: count_exceeds(2, session)
    then:
      action: escalate
      reason: "Agent '{$agent.id}' exceeded PII access threshold"
```

## Temporal Patterns

The `rate_exceeds` function counts matching facts within a sliding time window and fires when the rate passes a threshold. This enables detection of behavioral anomalies over time.

Example: "This agent's denial rate spiked 400% in the last 10 minutes -- escalate."

```yaml
rules:
  - name: denial-rate-spike
    when:
      denial_record:
        agent_id: $agent.id
        agent_id: rate_exceeds(10, 600)  # >10 denials in 600 seconds
    then:
      action: escalate
      reason: "Denial rate spike detected for agent '{$agent.id}'"
```

Time windows are specified in seconds. The function examines the `timestamp` slot of matching facts to determine which fall within the window.

## Cross-Fact Inference

Rules can match across multiple fact types simultaneously. When facts from different templates are present in working memory, the engine can detect patterns that span them.

Example: "Agent A passed data to Agent B. Agent B is requesting external network access. The combination violates information flow policy."

```yaml
rules:
  - name: information-flow-violation
    when:
      data_transfer:
        from_agent: $source.id
        to_agent: $requester.id
      network_request:
        agent_id: $requester.id
        destination: external
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Agent '{$requester.id}' received data from '{$source.id}' and is requesting external access"
```

Neither fact alone triggers the rule. Only when both exist in working memory at the same time does the pattern match fire.

## Working Memory Queries

Inspect working memory state without triggering rule evaluation using `engine.query()` and `engine.count()`.

```python
# Find all secret-clearance agents
agents = engine.query("agent", {"clearance": "secret"})

# Find all access logs for a specific agent
logs = engine.query("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha"})

# Count PII accesses without triggering rules
pii_count = engine.count("access_log", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha",
    "data_type": "PII"
})
```

These read from working memory directly. No rules fire, no side effects occur.

## Fact Lifecycle

**Assert** -- insert a single fact validated against its template:

```python
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "agent-alpha", "clearance": "secret", ...})
```

**Bulk assert** -- insert multiple facts in one call:

```python
engine.assert_facts([
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "hr_db", ...}),
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "finance_db", ...}),
])
```

**Retract** -- remove a fact from working memory:

```python
engine.retract("data_request", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "target": "hr_records"})
```

**Memory bounds**: working memory targets less than 5MB for 1000 facts per session. The CLIPS rete algorithm scales well within these bounds, keeping single-rule evaluation under 100 microseconds and 100-rule evaluation under 500 microseconds.

## Comparison to Stateless Engines

| Capability | OPA / Cedar | Fathom |
|---|---|---|
| Single request evaluation | Yes | Yes |
| Facts persist across evaluations | No | Yes (within session) |
| Cumulative reasoning | No -- each eval is isolated | Yes -- `count_exceeds` over session history |
| Temporal pattern detection | No | Yes -- `rate_exceeds` with time windows |
| Cross-fact inference | Limited -- single input document | Yes -- pattern match across all facts in memory |
| Memory model | Stateless | Stateful within session, stateless across sessions |

OPA evaluates a request against policy. Fathom reasons about a situation across multiple facts over time.

## Do Not

- Do not assume working memory persists across sessions. Each session starts with an empty working memory.
- Do not skip template validation. All facts are validated against their template schema at assertion time.
- Do not use `engine.evaluate()` when you only need to read state. Use `engine.query()` or `engine.count()` instead.
- Do not exceed 1000 facts per session without benchmarking. The 5MB memory target assumes this upper bound.
- Do not rely on fact insertion order for rule firing. Rules fire based on salience and module focus order, not assertion sequence.


## core/audit-log.md

# Runtime -- Audit Log

## What It Is

The Audit Log captures an immutable record of every evaluation. Each record includes the input facts, modules traversed, rules fired, decision reached, full reasoning trace, and duration. Records are structured JSON and append-only -- once written, they cannot be modified or deleted.

This provides the auditability required for governance, compliance, and debugging use cases. Every decision Fathom makes can be traced back to the exact facts and rules that produced it.

## What Gets Captured

Each evaluation produces one audit record containing:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `timestamp` | ISO 8601 string | When the evaluation occurred |
| `session_id` | string | Session that owns this evaluation |
| `input_facts` | array of objects | All facts in working memory at evaluation time |
| `modules_traversed` | array of strings | Modules entered during evaluation, in focus-stack order |
| `rules_fired` | array of strings | Rules that fired, in `module::rule` format |
| `decision` | string | Final outcome: `allow`, `deny`, `escalate`, `scope`, or `route` |
| `reason` | string | Human-readable explanation with variable interpolation applied |
| `duration_us` | integer | Evaluation duration in microseconds |
| `metadata` | object | Additional key-value pairs from rule `metadata` blocks |

## Log Format

Each record is a single JSON object, one per line (JSON Lines format).

```json
{
  "timestamp": "2025-01-15T10:30:00.047Z",
  "session_id": "sess-001",
  "input_facts": [
    {"template": "agent", "slots": {"id": "agent-alpha", "clearance": "secret", "purpose": "threat-analysis", "session_id": "sess-001"}},
    {"template": "data_request", "slots": {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "target": "hr_records", "classification": "top-secret", "action": "read"}}
  ],
  "modules_traversed": ["classification", "governance"],
  "rules_fired": [
    "classification::resolve-levels",
    "governance::deny-insufficient-clearance"
  ],
  "decision": "deny",
  "reason": "Agent clearance 'secret' insufficient for 'top-secret' data",
  "duration_us": 47,
  "metadata": {}
}
```

## Reasoning Trace

The `rules_fired` array is ordered by execution sequence. Combined with `modules_traversed`, it reconstructs the full reasoning path:

1. The engine entered the `classification` module first (per focus stack order).
2. The `resolve-levels` rule fired within that module.
3. The engine moved to the `governance` module.
4. The `deny-insufficient-clearance` rule fired, producing the `deny` decision.
5. Remaining modules (`routing`, `cumulative`) were never entered because the decision was reached.

This trace is accessible programmatically from the evaluation result:

```python
result = engine.evaluate()

print(result.rule_trace)    # ["classification::resolve-levels", "governance::deny-insufficient-clearance"]
print(result.module_trace)  # ["classification", "governance"]
print(result.duration_us)   # 47
```

## Append-Only Guarantee

Audit records are append-only. The log file is opened in append mode and records are written sequentially. There is no API to modify or delete records. This design supports:

- **Compliance audits** -- regulators can verify the log was not tampered with.
- **Incident investigation** -- every decision is preserved with its full context.
- **Debugging** -- reproduce any past evaluation by replaying its `input_facts`.

## Log Levels

Rules declare a `log` level in their `then` block:

| Level | What is Recorded |
|---|---|
| `none` | Rule firing is recorded in `rules_fired` but no additional detail |
| `summary` | Decision and reason are recorded |
| `full` | All fields captured, including complete `input_facts` snapshot |

The default log level is `summary` if not specified.

## Conventions

- Every evaluation produces exactly one audit record, regardless of how many rules fire.
- Audit records include the full fact snapshot at evaluation time, not just the facts that triggered rules.
- The `rules_fired` array preserves execution order. The first entry fired first.
- Use `full` logging for governance and compliance rules. Use `none` for high-frequency, low-risk evaluations.
- Store audit logs externally (filesystem, object storage, SIEM) for long-term retention.

## Do Not

- Do not modify or delete audit log records. The log is append-only by design.
- Do not disable audit logging for governance use cases. The audit trail is a compliance requirement.
- Do not store sensitive fact values (credentials, tokens) in working memory. They will appear in the audit log.
- Do not rely on audit logs for real-time monitoring. They are a historical record, not an event stream.
- Do not parse `reason` strings programmatically. Use `decision` and `metadata` for machine-readable outputs.


## core/attestation.md

# Runtime -- Attestation Service

## What It Is

The Attestation Service signs evaluation results with Ed25519 keys and produces JWT tokens that third parties can verify. It provides cryptographic proof that a specific evaluation occurred with specific inputs and produced a specific decision. The service is optional -- disabled by default, enabled for governance use cases.

## Why It Exists

Audit logs record what happened, but they are self-reported. The attestation service adds cryptographic non-repudiation: a signed token proves that a particular Fathom engine instance evaluated specific facts against specific rules and reached a specific decision. Third parties (auditors, partner systems, compliance tools) can verify this without trusting the Fathom operator.

Use cases:

- **Compliance attestation** -- prove to regulators that every agent action was governed by policy.
- **Cross-system trust** -- Bosun signs governance decisions that downstream systems verify before proceeding.
- **Audit chains** -- link signed evaluations into a verifiable sequence.

## How It Works

1. An evaluation completes, producing a result with decision, rule trace, and audit record.
2. The attestation service serializes the result into a canonical JSON payload.
3. The payload is signed with the engine's Ed25519 private key.
4. A JWT token is produced containing the payload and signature.
5. The token is returned alongside the evaluation result.

```python
result = engine.evaluate()

# When attestation is enabled on the rule:
result.attestation_token  # JWT string
result.decision           # "deny"
```

## Enabling Attestation

Attestation is controlled at the rule level via the `attestation` field in the `then` block:

```yaml
rules:
  - name: deny-insufficient-clearance
    salience: 100
    when:
      agent:
        clearance: below($data.classification)
      data_request:
        as: $data
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Clearance insufficient"
      attestation: true
      log: full
```

When `attestation: true`, the evaluation result includes a signed JWT. When `attestation: false` or omitted, no token is produced.

## JWT Token Contents

The JWT payload contains:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `iss` | Issuer -- identifies the Fathom engine instance |
| `iat` | Issued-at timestamp |
| `decision` | The evaluation decision (allow, deny, escalate, scope, route) |
| `rule_trace` | Ordered list of rules that fired |
| `input_hash` | SHA-256 hash of the input facts (privacy-preserving) |
| `session_id` | Session identifier |

## Verification

Third parties verify tokens using the engine's Ed25519 public key. The public key can be distributed via API endpoint, key registry, or out-of-band configuration.

```python
from fathom.attestation import verify_token

is_valid = verify_token(token, public_key)
# Returns True if signature is valid and payload is intact
```

## Key Management

- Ed25519 key pairs are generated per engine instance or per deployment.
- Private keys must be stored securely (environment variable, secrets manager).
- Public keys are freely distributable.
- Key rotation is the operator's responsibility. Fathom does not manage key lifecycle.

## Conventions

- Enable attestation only for governance and compliance rules where third-party verification is required.
- Use `log: full` alongside `attestation: true` to ensure the audit record captures the complete context.
- Distribute public keys through a secure, reliable channel.
- Store signed tokens alongside audit records for complete audit chains.

## Do Not

- Do not enable attestation on every rule. It adds overhead and is only needed for governance use cases.
- Do not embed private keys in rule files, configuration files, or source code.
- Do not parse JWT tokens manually. Use the `verify_token` function.
- Do not skip key rotation. Compromised keys invalidate all tokens signed with them.


## yaml/yaml-templates.md

# Runtime — YAML Template Authoring

Comprehensive reference for defining fact schemas as YAML templates. Templates compile to CLIPS `deftemplate` constructs and validate facts at assertion time.

## What It Is

A template declares the shape of a fact: its name, a human-readable description, and an ordered list of typed slots. The YAML compiler translates each template into a CLIPS `deftemplate`. At runtime, every `assert_fact` call is validated against the registered template, catching invalid slot names, disallowed values, and missing required slots before the fact reaches working memory.

## Template File Structure

Each YAML file contains a top-level `templates:` array. A single file may define one template or many related templates.

```yaml
# templates/agent.yaml
templates:
  - name: agent
    description: "An AI agent requesting access or action"
    slots:
      - name: id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: clearance
        type: symbol
        required: true
        allowed_values: [unclassified, cui, confidential, secret, top-secret]
      - name: purpose
        type: symbol
        required: true
      - name: session_id
        type: string
        required: true

  - name: data_request
    description: "A request to access a data source"
    slots:
      - name: agent_id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: target
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: classification
        type: symbol
        required: true
      - name: action
        type: symbol
        allowed_values: [read, write, delete]
        default: read

  - name: access_log
    description: "Record of a completed data access within a session"
    slots:
      - name: agent_id
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: source
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: data_type
        type: symbol
        required: true
      - name: timestamp
        type: float
```

**Required fields per template:** `name` and `slots`. The `description` field is optional but recommended.

**Required fields per slot:** `name` and `type`. The `required`, `allowed_values`, and `default` fields are optional.

## Slot Definition

Each slot accepts the following properties:

| Property | Type | Required | Purpose |
|----------|------|----------|---------|
| `name` | string | yes | Slot identifier, used when asserting facts |
| `type` | string | yes | Data type (see Supported Types below) |
| `required` | boolean | no | When `true`, facts must provide this slot |
| `allowed_values` | list | no | Restricts accepted values to this set |
| `default` | value | no | Value used when the fact omits this slot |

## Supported Types

| Type | CLIPS Mapping | Example Values |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| `string` | STRING | `"agent-alpha"`, `"sess-001"` |
| `symbol` | SYMBOL | `secret`, `read`, `PII` |
| `float` | FLOAT | `1713020400.0`, `3.14` |
| `integer` | INTEGER | `42`, `100` |

Use `symbol` for enumerated or categorical values (clearance levels, action types, data classifications). Use `string` for free-form identifiers. Use `float` for timestamps and decimal measurements. Use `integer` for counts and whole-number quantities.

## Validation Behavior

Fathom validates every fact against its template at assertion time. Three checks run before a fact enters working memory:

1. **Slot name validation.** Every key in the fact dict must match a declared slot name. Unknown slot names raise `ValidationError`.

```python
# Raises ValidationError -- "clearence" is not a valid slot
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "agent-alpha", "clearence": "secret"})
```

2. **Allowed values check.** When a slot declares `allowed_values`, the provided value must be in that list. Values outside the list raise `ValidationError`.

```python
# Raises ValidationError -- "cosmic" is not in allowed_values
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "agent-alpha", "clearance": "cosmic"})
```

3. **Required slots check.** When a slot has `required: true`, the fact must include it (unless a `default` is also declared). Missing required slots raise `ValidationError`.

## Allowed Values

Use `allowed_values` to constrain a symbol slot to a fixed set. This enforces controlled vocabularies (clearance levels, action types, data classifications). The YAML compiler generates CLIPS `(allowed-symbols ...)` constraints in the compiled `deftemplate`. See the `clearance` and `action` slots in the full example above.

## Default Values

When a slot declares `default` and the fact omits that slot, the default value is used. This applies during YAML-to-CLIPS compilation (as a CLIPS `(default ...)` clause) and during Python-side validation. See the `action` slot in `data_request` above: a fact asserted without `action` receives `read` automatically. If a slot is `required: true` and also has a `default`, the requirement is satisfied by the default.

## Raw CLIPS Passthrough

For advanced cases that the YAML abstraction cannot express, raw `deftemplate` strings can be loaded directly into the engine alongside YAML-defined templates. Both coexist in the same template registry.

```python
engine.load_clips_construct("""
(deftemplate custom-metric
    (slot name (type STRING))
    (slot value (type FLOAT))
    (multislot tags (type SYMBOL)))
""")
```

Use raw passthrough for CLIPS features not yet exposed in YAML, such as `multislot` declarations or complex default expressions. YAML-defined and raw-defined templates share the same working memory and can be referenced by the same rules.

## File Organization

Organize template files by domain. Each file groups related templates.

```
templates/
  agent.yaml        # agent, data_request, access_log
  data.yaml         # data sources, classifications
  governance.yaml   # policy decisions, audit records
```

Load all templates from a directory in one call:

```python
from fathom import Engine

engine = Engine()
engine.load_templates("templates/")
```

`load_templates` reads every `.yaml` file in the given directory, compiles each template to a CLIPS `deftemplate`, and registers it in the engine's template registry. Templates must be loaded before asserting facts of that type.

## Python API

| Method | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `engine.load_templates("templates/")` | Load and compile all YAML template files from directory |
| `engine.assert_fact("name", {slots})` | Validate against template, insert into working memory |
| `engine.assert_facts([(name, dict), ...])` | Bulk validate and insert multiple facts |
| `engine.query("name", {filter})` | Inspect working memory without triggering evaluation |
| `engine.retract("name", {filter})` | Remove matching facts from working memory |

## Conventions

- One template file per domain area (e.g., `agent.yaml` for agent-related templates).
- Always include `description` on templates for documentation and audit trail clarity.
- Use `symbol` type for any slot that will be compared in rules via classification-aware operators (`below`, `meets_or_exceeds`).
- Use `allowed_values` on every symbol slot where the value space is known and finite.
- Set `required: true` on slots that rules depend on for pattern matching. Missing slots cause silent match failures in CLIPS.
- Load templates before loading rules. Rules reference template slot names at compile time.
- Prefer YAML templates over raw CLIPS. Use raw passthrough only for features not expressible in YAML (e.g., `multislot`).

## Do Not

- Do not assert facts for a template that has not been loaded. The engine will raise `ValidationError`.
- Do not use slot names that differ only by case or punctuation. CLIPS slot names are case-sensitive and typos cause `ValidationError` at assertion time.
- Do not omit `type` on any slot. The YAML compiler requires it for CLIPS type mapping.
- Do not use `string` type for slots intended as classification levels or enums. Use `symbol` so that CLIPS operators and `allowed_values` constraints work correctly.
- Do not define the same template name in multiple YAML files. The second load overwrites the first without warning.
- Do not mix `multislot` logic into YAML templates. Use raw CLIPS passthrough for `multislot` declarations.


## yaml/yaml-facts.md

# Runtime — Fact Management

Comprehensive reference for asserting, querying, retracting, and reasoning about facts in Fathom's CLIPS-based working memory.

## What It Is

Facts are typed instances of templates asserted into working memory. They represent the current state of the world that rules reason about. Every fact conforms to a loaded template schema and is validated at assertion time. Facts persist within a session, enabling cumulative reasoning, temporal patterns, and cross-fact inference.

## Asserting Facts

Use `engine.assert_fact(template_name, slot_dict)` to insert a single fact into working memory.

```python
engine.assert_fact("agent", {
    "id": "agent-alpha",
    "clearance": "secret",
    "purpose": "threat-analysis",
    "session_id": "sess-001"
})

engine.assert_fact("data_request", {
    "agent_id": "agent-alpha",
    "target": "hr_records",
    "classification": "cui",
    "action": "read"
})
```

Each call validates the slot dictionary against the named template before inserting into CLIPS working memory. The `action` slot above defaults to `read` if omitted (per the template's `default` property).

## Bulk Assertion

Use `engine.assert_facts()` with a list of `(template_name, slot_dict)` tuples to insert multiple facts in a single call.

```python
engine.assert_facts([
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "hr_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020400.0}),
    ("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "finance_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020460.0}),
])
```

Bulk assertion validates each tuple against its template individually. If any tuple fails validation, the entire batch is rejected.

## Template Validation

Facts are validated against loaded templates at assertion time. Three categories of validation failure raise `ValidationError`:

- **Invalid slot names** -- A slot name not defined in the template (e.g., `"clearence"` instead of `"clearance"`).
- **Invalid allowed_values** -- A value outside the template's `allowed_values` constraint (e.g., `"cosmic"` for a clearance slot that only allows `[unclassified, cui, confidential, secret, top-secret]`).
- **Missing required slots** -- A required slot omitted from the slot dictionary.

```python
# Raises ValidationError -- "clearence" is not a valid slot
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "agent-alpha", "clearence": "secret"})

# Raises ValidationError -- "cosmic" is not in allowed_values
engine.assert_fact("agent", {"id": "agent-alpha", "clearance": "cosmic"})
```

Templates must be loaded before facts can be asserted against them. Load templates with `engine.load_templates("templates/")`.

## Working Memory Queries

Inspect working memory state without triggering rule evaluation.

**Query** returns matching facts:

```python
agents = engine.query("agent", {"clearance": "secret"})
logs = engine.query("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha"})
```

**Count** returns matching fact count:

```python
pii_count = engine.count("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "data_type": "PII"})
```

Both methods accept a `template_name` and an optional `filter_dict`. The filter matches facts whose slot values equal the provided values. Queries read working memory directly and never trigger the forward-chaining evaluation cycle.

## Fact Retraction

Remove facts from working memory with `engine.retract(template_name, filter_dict)`. All facts matching the filter are removed.

```python
engine.retract("data_request", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "target": "hr_records"})
```

Retracted facts no longer participate in pattern matching during subsequent evaluations.

## Fact Persistence

Working memory persists across evaluations within a session. This enables cumulative reasoning patterns that stateless engines (OPA, Cedar, Edictum) cannot express:

- **Cumulative reasoning:** "This agent has accessed PII from 3 sources this session -- deny the 4th."
- **Temporal patterns:** "This agent's denial rate spiked 400% in the last 10 minutes -- escalate."
- **Cross-fact inference:** "Agent A passed data to Agent B who is now requesting external network access -- the combination violates information flow policy."

Working memory is stateless across sessions. A new session starts with empty working memory.

```python
engine.assert_fact("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "hr_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020400.0})
engine.assert_fact("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "finance_db", "data_type": "PII", "timestamp": 1713020460.0})
engine.assert_fact("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha", "source": "medical_db", "data_type": "PHI", "timestamp": 1713020520.0})

# Rule using count_exceeds: "3 PII accesses in one session exceeds threshold"
result = engine.evaluate()  # -> escalate

logs = engine.query("access_log", {"agent_id": "agent-alpha"})
print(len(logs))  # 3
```

## Memory Bounds

Target: 1000 facts under 5MB per session. The CLIPS rete algorithm scales well within this bound -- 100-rule evaluations target under 500 microseconds, individual fact assertions under 10 microseconds.

## Fact Lifecycle in Evaluation

The forward-chaining evaluation cycle:

1. **Assert** -- Facts are asserted into working memory (via `assert_fact` or `assert_facts`, or by rule actions).
2. **Match** -- The rete network pattern-matches rules against the current fact set. Rules whose conditions are fully satisfied become activated.
3. **Select** -- The engine selects the highest-salience activated rule (conflict resolution). Module focus order determines which module's rules take precedence.
4. **Fire** -- The selected rule executes its action. Actions may assert new facts, retract existing facts, or produce output.
5. **Repeat** -- The cycle returns to step 2. New or retracted facts may activate or deactivate rules.
6. **Halt** -- The cycle ends when no more rules are activated (agenda is empty).

```python
result = engine.evaluate()

print(result.decision)     # "deny"
print(result.rule_trace)   # ["classification::resolve-levels", "governance::deny-insufficient-clearance"]
print(result.module_trace) # ["classification", "governance"]
print(result.duration_us)  # 47
```

The `rule_trace` shows every rule that fired. The `module_trace` shows which modules were reached before the decision resolved.

## Do Not

- Do not assert facts without loading templates first -- the engine cannot validate untyped facts.
- Do not assume working memory carries across sessions -- each session starts empty.
- Do not use `query` or `count` to trigger evaluation -- they are read-only inspections. Call `engine.evaluate()` to run the forward-chaining cycle.
- Do not exceed 1000 facts or 5MB per session without profiling -- performance targets assume this bound.
- Do not rely on fact insertion order for rule firing priority -- use salience and module focus order instead.


## yaml/yaml-rule-language.md

# Platform -- YAML Rule Language

## What It Is

The YAML rule language is the primary authoring format for Fathom rules. Rules authored in YAML compile to CLIPS `defrule` constructs at load time. The language provides comparison operators, classification-aware operators, temporal operators, cross-fact references, and a structured action system.

## Rule Structure

Every rule file has a top-level envelope and a `rules` array.

```yaml
ruleset: access-control       # Logical name for this collection
version: 1.0                  # Semantic version of the ruleset
module: governance             # Target module (controls focus-stack ordering)

rules:
  - name: deny-insufficient-clearance
    description: "Deny when agent clearance is below data classification"
    salience: 100
    when:
      agent:
        clearance: below($data.classification)
      data_request:
        as: $data
    then:
      action: deny
      reason: "Agent clearance '{agent.clearance}' insufficient for '{$data.classification}' data"
      log: full
```

**Top-level fields:**

| Field     | Required | Purpose                                          |
|-----------|----------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `ruleset` | yes      | Logical name grouping related rules              |
| `version` | yes      | Version of this ruleset                          |
| `module`  | yes      | CLIPS module these rules compile into            |
| `rules`   | yes      | Array of rule definitions                        |

**Per-rule fields:**

| Field         | Required | Purpose                                       |
|---------------|----------|-----------------------------------------------|
| `name`        | yes      | Unique identifier within the module           |
| `description` | no       | Human-readable explanation                    |
| `salience`    | no       | Integer priority (higher fires first)         |
| `when`        | yes      | Pattern-matching conditions against facts     |
| `then`        | yes      | Actions to execute when conditions match      |

## Comparison Operators
